step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.

Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.

Therapist:
step7_response: Based on our conversation, I think it would be helpful for you to write down your reasons for quitting smoking. When you feel overwhelmed by cravings or stress, look at that list and remind yourself why you wanted to quit in the first place. It can be a small piece of paper or even a note on your phone, but having those reasons written down can help motivate you to stay on track. Would you be willing to try writing down your reasons for quitting and keeping them somewhere visible?
